dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 7 responding for overdose/poisoning/ingestion in Richmond.
Hello, my name is Jordan, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today, ma'am?
I took a lot of Seroquel, and now I feel really strange. I called because I felt bad after taking it.
Ma'am, can you tell me your full name, and when is your birthday, please?
My name is Maria, and I was born in May.
What city are we in right now, Maria?
We are in Richmond. That's where they sent the ambulance, right?
Eye opening, response to verbal, and motor response all are normal. Glasgow Coma Scale is 15.
Resp rate at 20, good breath sounds.
Pulse is strong, and peripheral pulses present-all sides.
Maria, can you describe to me what you're feeling right now?
I feel like my heart is racing, I feel sleepy and a little nauseated but haven't vomited.
Do you have any known medical conditions or prior health issues you'd like to tell us about?
Yes, I have high blood pressure, type 1 diabetes, asthma, and I had a previous heart attack.
What medications do you currently take, Maria?
Lantus, NovoLog, Valium, Prozac, Seroquel, and Eliquis.
What was going on right before you took all the Seroquel, Maria?
I lost my granddaughter back to her mother recently. I felt so sad and wanted the emotional pain to end. I made a decision to take the pills. That was the plan.
Did you take the pills with anything? Water? Food?
I took them with water, about an hour ago.
You reported elevated blood sugar and no chest pain, dizziness, or breathing issues.
Pulse: 124 beats per minute. Fast but regular.
SpO2 is 99 percent on room air.
Blood pressure is 119 over 80-normal upper number, lower one is fine.
Respiration rate is 20 per minute-normal.
Blood sugar is high-we read 251 mg/dl on the meter.
You did the EKG first-confirmed it was sinus tach with old infarct?
Yes, and we just reconfirmed with the 12-lead EKG. Still no acute changes, but she had a prior infarct. Left atrial enlargement may also be present.
4-lead ECG monitoring has been started for ongoing cardiac surveillance.
IV has been placed-extremity vein, in the left arm.
We have ordered the 12-lead EKG and placed the IV for monitoring and possible support.
Skin is normal, no rashes or unusual bruising noted.
Eyes are open, and she's answering questions-no signs of confusion or disorientation. She is alert and oriented to four.
She can walk with some help. No deformity or angulation observed.
Maria reports no chest pain at this time.
Airway is clear, patent, and she doesn't need any assistance to maintain it.
Breathing remains stable, no signs of fatigue or work of breathing.
How would you rate the physical discomfort you're feeling right now, Maria?
Zero, I guess. I just feel like I might throw up, but no actual pain yet.
She feels a bit more nauseous now, but no vomiting. No abdominal pain reported.
Given a suspected suicide attempt with Seroquel overdose, I'm starting emergency protocol 7-overdose/poisoning.
Agree with the protocol. IV, EKG, and vitals are covered.
Her pulse is now down to 117-still tachycardic.
Respiration is at 18 per minute and normal.
Blood pressure is lower now at 118 over 70.
SpO2 is 100 percent, no dyspnea. Her glucose is now zero.
Continue monitoring with 4 and 12-lead EKG. IV remains patent for possible fluids.
Maria, are you still feeling nauseous? Still no real pain?
Yes, I still feel like I might throw up, but no actual pain yet.
She continues to report no pain, abdominal discomfort, or chest tightness.
She described major depression since losing custody of her 7-month-old granddaughter. Notably, she is on Eliquis (apixaban) and was prescribed Seroquel for depression.
Maria, we need to go to Chippenham Hospital now-are you okay with that?
Yes, I agree. I will go where you think best.
IV lock is secure, and EKG is stable. Transporting to hospital now.
Chippenham is the recommended facility for this presentation.
Any known drug allergies, Maria?
No known allergies to medications.
You also mentioned Eliquis-do you take it for any specific purposes?
It's for blood thinning after a prior heart issue.
When was your last meal before taking the pills?
I had a light snack-maybe four hours before taking the pills.
Airway is patent and patent-able. Preferred posture: Semi-Fowler's for comfort.
Respiration rate remains at 18 and oxygen saturation meets normal level.
Skin is pink and warm. Capillary refill is <2 seconds.
She has a history of primary hypertension, type 1 diabetes, and old myocardial infarction.
We noted old infarct on the EKG-done on arrival. No acute changes.
Nausea is new since en route, but no acute signs of GI bleeding or dehydration.
First reading was 251 and then dropped to zero in the second check. Monitor closely for hypoglycemia.
Ensure IV remains patent to allow for glucose administration if needed.
No medications given yet. Confirm protocol excludes activated charcoal or naloxone for this overdose.
Agreed-no naloxone or antiemetics indicated per protocol unless symptoms progress.
No petechiae or ecchymosis noted-nothing suggesting acid-base imbalance.
No lymphadenopathy or neck masses.
No significant findings-clear mucous membranes seen.
Heart sounds are regular, tachycardia present per EKG.
Abdominal scan-soft and non-tender. No rigidity or distention.
No abnormal color or frequency in urination.
Chippenham is the designated facility for this transport.
She's secure on the stretcher. IV, EKG, and vitals are all monitored during transport.
No new pain reported. Nausea persists-monitor for vomiting or change in mental status.
Maria, please confirm your pain level again.
Still zero, no real pain but still feel nauseous.
BP is 118/70, stable during transport.
IV remains secure, and we have a medical report ready for the hospital.
Comfort is low but manageable-no need for sedation or analgesia at this time.
Patient is alert, oriented, and no new symptoms.
She is maintaining on her current medications including Eliquis for its anticoagulation.
Wheelchair assistance on standby per hospital instructions.
Hospital has been informed of our estimated time of arrival and the patient's status.
No acute findings on second EKG remeasurement.
No acute distress reported, and oxygen remains at 100%.
Pain management is not needed due to stable status and no progression.
She does not use Eliquis today, correct?
No, I take it daily.
That's important to note for ED providers, as anticoagulation can affect management.
Protocol followed appropriately for overdose per patient and medical history.
Occupancy: One EMS transport, no other agencies involved.
Completed chaining process. All patient equipment and IV secure.
Transporting safely. Nausea-monitor for deterioration.
Transferred patient condition log.
